title: Unveiling the Triumph: A Reflection on CppIndiaCon 2023's Spectacular Journey
author: Dheeraj Jha
excerpt: A first-hand account of CppIndiaCon 2023 — the highlights, key talks, hallway conversations, and what makes India's annual C++ festival truly special.
body:
## The C++ Festival of India

CppIndia is a dynamic community that thrives on the shared love for C++, providing a welcoming space for developers to learn, collaborate, and engage in insightful discussions about the language.

CppIndiaCon 2023 was the third edition of our annual conference — a two-day virtual event held on **August 4–5, 2023**. Planning began in January 2023, and by the time the event arrived, we had assembled 22 volunteers, evaluated over 20 talk proposals, and opened registrations in March.

## By the Numbers

The response from the community was humbling:

- **577 total registrations**
- Day 1: 423 attendees
- Day 2: 194 attendees

These numbers reflect not just interest in C++, but a growing appetite for community-led, accessible technical education in India.

## Day 1 — August 4, 2023

### Mike Shah — Optimization Design Patterns _(Avg Rating: 4.62)_

Mike opened the conference with a session on optimization design patterns that set the tone for the two days ahead. Referencing Donald Knuth's classic principle — *"Premature optimization is the root of all evil"* — he walked through practical, measured approaches to making C++ code faster without sacrificing readability.

### Kiran Kannappan — Introduction to Intel oneAPI _(Avg Rating: 4.17)_

Kiran from Intel gave attendees a practical introduction to the oneAPI unified programming model, showing how it simplifies heterogeneous computing across CPUs, GPUs, and FPGAs using modern C++.

### Jonathan Müller — C++ Features You Might Not Know _(Avg Rating: 4.14)_

Jonathan, always a crowd favourite, surfaced lesser-known C++ features that even seasoned developers miss. His delivery was sharp and the examples immediately applicable.

### Venkata Naga Ravikiran Bulusu — Understanding Common Concurrency Patterns _(Avg Rating: 4.22)_

Concurrency remains one of C++'s most nuanced domains. Venkata's session broke down common patterns — producer-consumer, thread pools, and synchronization primitives — with clarity and practical examples.

### Bryce Lelbach (Keynote) — AI-Assisted Programming _(Avg Rating: 4.36)_

Bryce delivered the Day 1 keynote on AI-assisted programming, exploring how tools like ChatGPT are changing how developers write and reason about code — and where C++ expertise still matters most.

## Day 2 — August 5, 2023

### Ankur Satle — Value-Oriented Design by Example _(Avg Rating: 4.43)_

Ankur's talk on value semantics and design was one of the most discussed sessions of the conference. He built a compelling case for value-oriented APIs through concrete, relatable examples.

### Andreas Fertig — C++ Coroutines from Scratch _(Avg Rating: 4.42)_

Coroutines are powerful but notoriously hard to grasp. Andreas started from first principles and walked attendees through building coroutine-based abstractions step by step — a rare kind of clarity on this topic.

### Kushashwa Ravi Shrimali — Using C++ for AI Solutions _(Avg Rating: 4.52)_

Kushashwa's session connected C++ with the AI world — showing how modern C++ powers inference pipelines and AI toolkits, making it highly relevant to the current engineering landscape.

### Selvakumar Jawahar — Why 5G is Fast? & C++ in 5G Open Source _(Avg Rating: 4.15)_

A unique session that explored C++'s role in the 5G ecosystem — from open-source implementations to the real-time performance requirements that make C++ the right tool for the job.

## Lightning Talks

Our lightning talk segment featured three community members:

- **Prince Gupta** — Taking Advantage of Auto Vectorization
- **Emmanuel Danso Nyarko** — Understanding Dynamic Allocators for Optimization in Containers
- **Mahendra Garodi** — Standard C++ Attributes

Lightning talks are always a highlight — they're proof that great ideas don't need an hour to land.

## The Team Behind It

None of this happens without volunteers:

**Q&A Hosts:** Sparsh, Subhash, Kashish, Krishnan 
**Event Organizers:** Kashish, Hemil (Trivia), Harshal, Thilak (Social Media) 
**Backend Team:** Jayshree, Subhash, Niket 
**Core Leadership:** Mahendra, Ankur

## Gold Sponsors

We are grateful to our 2023 gold sponsors:

- **Intel Software**
- **Qube Cinema**
- **think-cell**
- **Bloomberg LP**

their support made the conference free and accessible to everyone.

## What's Next

CppIndiaCon 2024 is already in motion. With speakers like Alex Dathskovsky, Jonathan Müller, Mike Shah, and Pavithra Ashok Kumar confirmed, and sponsors MulticoreWare, think-cell, and Bloomberg LP on board — the fourth edition promises to be bigger and better.

If CppIndiaCon 2023 taught us anything, it's that the Indian C++ community is not just active — it's exceptional. See you next year.

title: std::optional Gets Superpowers in C++23: Monadic Operations
author: Jayshree Ambekar
excerpt: C++23 adds transform, and_then, and or_else to std::optional — turning error-prone null checks into clean, chainable pipelines.

title: std::pmr: The Allocator Revolution Nobody Talks About
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: Polymorphic Memory Resources let you swap allocators at runtime without changing container types. Here's why that's a bigger deal than it sounds.

title: C++ Attributes: [[nodiscard]], [[likely]], and the Ones You're Missing
author: Jayshree Ambekar
excerpt: C++ attributes are more than just [[nodiscard]]. From branch prediction hints to ABI control, here's the full picture of what they unlock.

title: if consteval: The Missing Link in Compile-Time C++
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: std::is_constant_evaluated() was broken by design. C++23's if consteval fixes it — and unlocks a pattern that makes constexpr code truly bifurcatable.

title: Structured Bindings: Beyond std::pair and std::tuple
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: Everyone uses structured bindings for pair and tuple. Almost nobody knows you can make any type work with them — or what happens under the hood.

title: Small String Optimization: The Hidden Trick Inside std::string
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: std::string stores short strings without any heap allocation. Understanding SSO lets you write dramatically faster string-heavy code.

title: Deducing `this` in C++23 — A Complete Guide
author: Ankur M Satle
excerpt: C++23's deducing this finally eliminates the need for CRTP in many patterns. Here's how it works and when to use it.

title: Ranges in C++20: A Practical Introduction
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: std::ranges transforms the way we write algorithms. This guide walks through filter, transform, and custom views with real examples.

title: Trip Report: CppCon 2024
author: Dheeraj Jha
excerpt: A first-hand account of CppCon 2024 — highlights, key talks, hallway conversations, and what the C++ community is excited about.
body:
## CppCon 2024 — Aurora, Colorado

CppCon 2024 took place in September at the Gaylord Rockies Resort in Aurora, Colorado. After attending remotely for two years, this was my first in-person CppCon, and it did not disappoint.

## The Conference Format

CppCon runs five full days with six parallel tracks. The sheer volume of content is overwhelming in the best way — you will miss talks you wanted to see, and that is okay. The recordings appear on YouTube within weeks.

## Keynotes

**Bjarne Stroustrup** opened with his now-traditional look at where C++ is headed. His focus this year was on safety — responding to the US government's push to move away from memory-unsafe languages. His message: C++ can be safe if you use it right, and C++26's safety profiles will make that easier.

**Herb Sutter's** talk on CppFront (his experimental C++ syntax superset) drew the largest crowd of the week. Whether or not CppFront ships, the ideas it explores — especially around lifetime safety — are influencing the standard.

## Talks I Attended

### "What Hana Dusíková Has Done for C++" — Lightning Talks
Hana's constexpr regex work continues to push the boundaries of compile-time computation. Her demos of compile-time string processing left the room speechless.

### "Sender/Receiver in Practice"
With `std::execution` coming in C++26, this was one of the most practically useful talks. The sender/receiver model for async work is finally clicking for me after this session.

### "Concurrency Patterns for Modern C++"
A deep-dive into lock-free data structures. The presenter showed how wrong most "lock-free" code on the internet actually is, and demonstrated correct implementations with formal proofs.

## Hallway Conversations

The best part of CppCon is not the talks — it is the hallway track. I had conversations with compiler engineers, library authors, and WG21 paper authors. The C++ community is remarkably open and generous with their time.

I spoke with several people about CppIndia. The universal reaction was enthusiasm — people in the global community want to see a thriving Indian C++ scene.

## What the Community Is Excited About

1. **C++26 Reflection** — Static reflection is coming and everyone is building mental models of what it enables
2. **Safety Profiles** — The industry pressure is real; C++ is responding
3. **std::execution** — Async programming is about to change fundamentally
4. **Carbon Language** — Not a replacement, but a migration path experiment worth watching

## Bringing It Home

Every CppCon I attend, I come back with enough ideas for a year of CppIndia talks. If you have never been and can make it happen, do it. If you cannot, the YouTube recordings are excellent and free.

See you at CppIndiaCon 2025.

title: Memory Safety in C++ — The 2025 State of Affairs
author: Mahendra Garodi
excerpt: With Rust's rise and the White House memo, memory safety is the hottest topic in C++. Here's where we actually stand.
body:
## The Political Context

In 2024, the US White House released a memo urging developers to move away from memory-unsafe languages. C and C++ were explicitly named. This sent ripples through the industry.

Is C++ dying? No. But the pressure is real, and the C++ community is responding.

## What Does "Memory Safe" Mean?

Memory safety refers to preventing a class of bugs:

- **Buffer overflows** — writing past the end of an array
- **Use-after-free** — using a pointer after the memory is freed
- **Dangling references** — returning a reference to a local variable
- **Data races** — concurrent access without synchronization
- **Integer overflow** — silent wrapping that corrupts values

Rust prevents all of these at compile time via its borrow checker. C++ does not — but the gap is narrowing.

## What Modern C++ Already Gives You

If you write modern C++, you already avoid most of these:

```cpp
// Unsafe C
char* buf = malloc(10);
buf[15] = 'x'; // buffer overflow — undefined behaviour
free(buf);
buf[0] = 'y'; // use-after-free — undefined behaviour

// Modern C++ — these problems cannot happen
auto buf = std::vector<char>(10);
buf.at(15) = 'x'; // throws std::out_of_range
// buf is automatically freed when it goes out of scope
// no raw pointer, so no use-after-free possible
```

The key tools:
- `std::vector`, `std::array` — bounds-checked containers
- `std::unique_ptr`, `std::shared_ptr` — automatic memory management
- `std::span` — safe non-owning views
- `std::string_view` — safe string references

## C++26 Safety Profiles

Herb Sutter and the safety subgroup are working on **Safety Profiles** for C++26. A profile is a set of rules that, when followed, guarantees a certain class of bugs cannot occur.

For example, a bounds-safety profile would:
- Warn on all raw array indexing
- Require `.at()` or explicit bounds checks
- Flag pointer arithmetic

Profiles are opt-in per translation unit, enabling gradual adoption.

## Sanitizers: Your Safety Net Today

While waiting for C++26, use sanitizers in your test builds:

```cmake
target_compile_options(myapp PRIVATE
    -fsanitize=address,undefined
)
target_link_options(myapp PRIVATE
    -fsanitize=address,undefined
)
```

**AddressSanitizer** catches: buffer overflows, use-after-free, heap corruption  
**UBSanitizer** catches: undefined behaviour, signed overflow, null dereference

These do not catch everything at compile time like Rust does, but they catch bugs at runtime during testing — which is when you want to find them.

## The Realistic Outlook

C++ will not get Rust's borrow checker — the designs are too different and backward compatibility is sacrosanct. But C++26 safety profiles, combined with modern idioms and sanitizers, will make C++ significantly safer than it is today.

For new safety-critical greenfield projects, Rust is a legitimate choice. For the billions of lines of existing C++ code, modern C++ with profiles is the path forward.

The language is evolving. The community is engaged. C++ in 2026 is meaningfully safer than C++ in 2016.
